levels.body: In earlier manuscripts, “blood-dimmed tide” was “‘the blood stained flood”’ [NLI 13,588 (14), 4r] (Yeats 155). The tide that is loosened is made dim with blood.
worksCited: Yeats, W. B. *Michael Robartes and the Dancer: Manuscript Materials*. Edited by Thomas Francis Parkinson and Anne Brannen, Cornell University Press, 1994.

levels.body:
The change from stained to dimmed points to a dark future of the world as the meaning of dimmed is “rendered dim” (“Dimmed, *Adj*.”).

“Blood-dimmed tide” refers to the forces of death and destruction that are unleashed at the end of an era ( see [[the-second-coming]]). As the era is coming to an end the forces are growing stronger (Ellmann 258-59).
The noun “tide” evokes the feeling of overwhelming power, a natural force of destruction. This force is so strong that it will overwhelm the world, and the chaos and destruction that ensue cannot be stopped anymore.
worksCited: “Dimmed, *Adj*.” *Oxford English Dictionary*, Oxford UP, June 2025, doi.org/10.1093/OED/2798170032. 26.09.2025.
Ellmann, Richard. *The Identity of Yeats by Richard Ellmann*. Farber and Farber, 1983.

levels.body: This phrase, in addition, refers to the contemporary world situation – more specifically WWI, the rising forces of fascism, and the Russian Revolution. The latter had a major impact on Yeats, particularly the brutal massacre of the Romanov family. In this context, the tide can also be read as representing the revolutionaries, the Bolsheviks (Ellmann 257-58).
worksCited: Ellmann, Richard. *The Identity of Yeats by Richard Ellmann*. Faber and Faber, 1983.

levels.body: Yeats uses a similar expression in his later poem “The Gyres”: “Irrational streams of blood are staining earth” (line 5), similarly indicating the chaos and destruction of the world.
